---
title: "Exercise 1: Foundations of data analytics"
---

## Preface

The purpose of this document is to give an introduction to data analytics with Python.
We will use three main libraries for data analytics provided for Python. For special cases,
we will use additional libraries as mentioned in the text.

The three main libraries are:

1. NumPy  
2. Pandas  
3. Scikit-learn  

## NumPy

NumPy is an acronym for “Numeric Python” or “Numerical Python”. It is a library that
provides a set of array and matrix data types which are essential for statistics, econometrics,
and data analysis. They behave similarly to the data structures in Python. In addition,
NumPy offers methods to manipulate and extract information from these structures.

An introduction to NumPy can be found in the slides “Part 2: Introduction to Python” of
the course “Introduction to Programming” (first semester Bachelor at Frankfurt School).

## Pandas

Pandas is a Python library that provides high-level data structures and a vast variety of tools
for analysis. The great feature of this package is the ability to translate rather complex
operations with data into one or two commands. Pandas contains many built-in methods
for grouping, filtering, and combining data, as well as time-series functionality. All of this
is followed by impressive speed indicators.

In Chapter 1, an introduction to using DataFrames with Pandas is given.

## Scikit-learn

Scikit-learn provides a range of supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms via a
consistent interface in Python. The library is focused on modeling data. It is not focused on
loading, manipulating, and summarizing data. For these features, refer to NumPy and
Pandas.

The structure of the implementation of the algorithms always follows the same template,
so that they can be used in the same way. Example for the implementation of a supervised
learning algorithm:

```{python}
# This is a schematic example to illustrate a common estimator pattern.

class ModelEstimator(object):
    def __init__(self, param1="pvalue1"):
        self.param1 = param1

    def fit(self, X, y):
        # X are input values, y are target values (supervised case)
        # here the functionality for model estimation is implemented
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        # implementation of the predicting function
        return X
```


## DataFrames with Pandas

### DataFrames

While a matrix is of simple structure intended for mathematical operations, a DataFrame is
more suited to representing a table of data. It is a list of vectors of equal length. The top line
of a DataFrame, called the header, contains the column names. Each horizontal line afterward
denotes a data row.

```{python}
import pandas as pd

data = {
    "employee": ["John Doe", "Peter Gynn", "Jolie Hope"],
    "salary": [21000, 23400, 26800],
    "startdate": ["2010-11-1", "2008-3-25", "2007-3-14"],
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df
```

```{python}
df.dtypes
```

```{python}
df.iloc[0,1]
```

```{python}
df.loc[1:2, ["employee","salary"]]
```

```{python}
df.loc[len(df)] = ["Mike Tyson", 19400, "2018-6-27"]
df
```

### Import and Export

```{python}
# data = pd.read_csv("filename.csv")
# data = pd.read_excel("filename.xlsx")
# data.to_csv("output.csv", index=False)
```

### Basic Statistical Functions for DataFrames

A large number of methods compute basic statistical measures on a DataFrame.
Most of them are aggregations such as `sum` and `mean`.

```{python}
# data.sum()
```

```{python}
# data.iloc[:, 1:7].cumsum()
```

### Common statistical functions in Pandas

| Function     | Description                             |
|-------------|------------------------------------------|
| `count()`   | Number of non-null observations          |
| `sum()`     | Sum of values                            |
| `mean()`    | Mean of values                           |
| `median()`  | Median of values                         |
| `mode()`    | Mode of values                           |
| `std()`     | Standard deviation of the values         |
| `min()`     | Minimum value                            |
| `max()`     | Maximum value                            |
| `abs()`     | Absolute value                           |
| `prod()`    | Product of values                        |
| `cumsum()`  | Cumulative sum                           |
| `cumprod()` | Cumulative product                       |


## Example case

- Get a dataset (nice datasource)
- Import and prepare
- Produce a notebook

::: {.callout-note title="TODO"}
**TODO: add a setup explanation + tests for available python version and packages**

**TODO: add a short exercise for downloading a dataset, and running common statistical functions (preparing a first jupyter notebook report)**

**TODO : add a summary**
:::
